title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Focaccia Genovese Recipe: Authentic Rosemary Olive Oil Bread
seo.metaDescription: Bake authentic Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil. This traditional Italian bread recipe brings the taste of Liguria to your home. Easy to follow!
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with Rosemary & Olive Oil
h2Headings: Ingredients for Rosemary Olive Oil Focaccia
Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Focaccia Genovese
seo.imageAlt: Authentic Focaccia Genovese with fresh rosemary and olive oil, baked to golden perfection.
seo.imageCaption: Golden, dimpled Focaccia Genovese, fresh from the oven, drizzled with olive oil and sprinkled with rosemary, ready to serve.

description: Experience the authentic taste of Liguria with this traditional Focaccia Genovese. This beloved Italian flatbread is characterized by its airy, chewy texture, golden crust, and generous dimples filled with aromatic rosemary and rich olive oil. Perfect as an appetizer, side, or light meal, it brings a piece of Italian culinary heritage to your table.

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large bowl, combine the warm water and active dry yeast. Let it sit for about 5 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active. This crucial step ensures a light and airy focaccia.

instructions.title: Mix the Dough
instructions.text: Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Stir with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms. Gradually add 30ml of the extra virgin olive oil, continuing to mix until it is fully incorporated.

instructions.title: Knead and First Proof
instructions.text: Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for about 10 minutes until it is smooth and elastic. Place the dough in a clean, lightly oiled bowl, turning once to coat. Cover with plastic wrap and let it rise in a warm place for 1 to 1.5 hours, or until doubled in size. This long first rise develops deep flavor, though the time listed is for active work.

instructions.title: Prepare Pan and Stretch Dough
instructions.text: Generously grease a 9x13 inch baking pan with the remaining 20ml of olive oil. Gently transfer the risen dough to the pan, being careful not to deflate it too much. Gently stretch the dough to fill the pan, or as close as possible without tearing. If it resists, let it rest for 10 minutes, then continue stretching.

instructions.title: Dimple and Second Proof
instructions.text: Using your fingertips, create deep dimples all over the surface of the dough. Drizzle generously with more olive oil and sprinkle with the chopped fresh rosemary and flaky sea salt. Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap and let it rise again for 30-45 minutes in a warm place, or until visibly puffy. This is another passive rise.

instructions.title: Bake the Focaccia
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 220C (425F). Bake the focaccia for 20-25 minutes, or until golden brown on top and cooked through. The bottom should also be nicely browned. Keep an eye on it to prevent over-browning.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Once baked, remove the focaccia from the oven. Immediately drizzle with a final touch of high-quality extra virgin olive oil for extra flavor and shine. Let it cool slightly in the pan before transferring to a cutting board. Slice into squares or strips and serve warm.

tips: For the most authentic flavor, use a high-quality extra virgin olive oil, especially for the final drizzle. It makes a significant difference.
Do not rush the rising process; patience is key for developing the focaccia's characteristic airy texture and deep flavor. A warm, draft-free spot is ideal for proofing.
Focaccia is best served warm, fresh from the oven. If storing, reheat briefly in a preheated oven at 180C (350F) for 5-7 minutes to restore its crispness.
article:
Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil: A Taste of Ligurian Perfection

Imagine the sun-drenched coast of Liguria, Italy, where the air is fragrant with sea salt and wild herbs. It's here that one of Italy's most beloved and deceptively simple breads was born: Focaccia Genovese. More than just a flatbread, this iconic bake is a testament to the power of a few high-quality ingredients, expertly combined to create something truly extraordinary. With its crisp, golden crust, airy, olive oil-rich interior, and the irresistible aroma of fresh rosemary and sea salt, Focaccia Genovese is a culinary masterpiece that transports you straight to the heart of Genoa. If you’ve ever dreamed of recreating that authentic Italian bakery experience in your own kitchen, then mastering the Focaccia Genovese Recipe is a delightful journey worth embarking on.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?

Focaccia Genovese, or *Fûgassa* in the local Ligurian dialect, is not just any focaccia. It stands apart from its thicker, often more bread-like cousins found elsewhere in Italy. Hailing specifically from Genoa, the capital of Liguria, this focaccia is characterized by its distinctively flat profile, high hydration dough, and an unapologetically generous use of extra virgin olive oil – both kneaded into the dough and drizzled lavishly on top before baking.

The true magic of Genovese focaccia lies in its texture: a delicate balance of a thin, remarkably crispy bottom crust, a slightly chewy, impossibly airy crumb riddled with large, irregular holes, and a glistening, golden-brown top studded with coarse sea salt and fragrant rosemary sprigs. Those signature dimples aren't just for show; they're created to hold pools of precious olive oil and saline solution, ensuring every bite is bursting with flavor and moisture. It’s traditionally enjoyed for breakfast with coffee, as a snack, or alongside various meals, embodying the simple, yet profound, culinary philosophy of Liguria.

## Ingredients for Rosemary Olive Oil Focaccia

The beauty of a truly authentic Focaccia Genovese Recipe lies in its simplicity. High-quality ingredients are paramount, as there are so few of them to hide behind.

*   **Strong Bread Flour (Type 0 or 00):** About 500g. A high-protein flour is essential for developing good gluten structure, which helps create that characteristic airy, chewy crumb.
*   **Warm Water:** Around 400-425ml (about 80-85% hydration). The high hydration is key to the focaccia's light texture.
*   **Active Dry Yeast:** 7g (or 2.5 tsp). This is what gives the bread its lift and airy structure.
*   **Fine Sea Salt:** 10g (about 2 tsp) for the dough. Salt is crucial for flavor and controlling yeast activity.
*   **Sugar (Optional):** 5g (about 1 tsp). A tiny bit of sugar can help "wake up" the yeast, especially if you're unsure of its freshness.
*   **High-Quality Extra Virgin Olive Oil:** At least 60-80ml for the dough, plus more for the pan and liberal drizzling on top (another 40-50ml). This is the soul of Focaccia Genovese, so invest in a good one with a fruity, peppery flavor.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** A few sprigs, leaves stripped and roughly chopped. Its aromatic notes are quintessential.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt:** For sprinkling on top. This adds a delightful textural crunch and bursts of salinity.

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Focaccia Genovese

Crafting the perfect Focaccia Genovese Recipe is a patient and rewarding process.

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine the warm water, yeast, and optional sugar. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until frothy. This indicates the yeast is active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Stir with a sturdy spoon or your hands until a shaggy, sticky dough forms. Add about 2 tablespoons of the extra virgin olive oil and continue to mix until it's mostly incorporated. The dough will be very wet – resist the urge to add more flour.
3.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp tea towel and let it rise in a warm spot for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until doubled in size. During this time, perform 2-3 "stretch and fold" cycles every 30-40 minutes. To do this, lightly oil your hands, gently grab an edge of the dough, stretch it upwards, and fold it over itself towards the center. Rotate the bowl and repeat 3-4 times. This builds gluten strength without intense kneading.
4.  **Prepare the Pan:** Liberally oil a 9x13 inch baking pan (or a similar size, a quarter sheet pan is ideal) with about 2-3 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil. Ensure the sides are well coated.
5.  **Transfer and Shape:** Gently pour or scrape the risen dough into the prepared pan. With oiled hands, gently stretch and press the dough to mostly fill the pan. Don't force it; if it resists, let it rest for 10 minutes, then continue.
6.  **Second Rise (Proofing):** Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap or a damp cloth and let it rise again for another 45-60 minutes, or until visibly puffy.
7.  **Dimple and Season:** Preheat your oven to 220°C (425°F). Once the dough is proofed, make the signature dimples. Dip your fingers in olive oil and firmly press them all over the surface of the dough, creating deep indentations without tearing through to the bottom. Don't be shy!
8.  **The Brine (Optional but Recommended):** In a small bowl, whisk together 2 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil with 2 tablespoons of water and a pinch of fine sea salt. Drizzle this mixture generously over the dimpled focaccia. This helps keep the focaccia moist and adds a salty kick.
9.  **Final Toppings:** Drizzle with another generous amount of extra virgin olive oil, sprinkle with the chopped fresh rosemary, and finish with coarse sea salt.
10. **Bake:** Bake for 20-25 minutes, or until the focaccia is golden brown on top and deeply golden on the bottom. If your oven bakes unevenly, rotate the pan halfway through.
11. **Cool:** Once baked, immediately transfer the focaccia from the pan to a wire rack to cool slightly. This prevents the bottom from becoming soggy. Serve warm.

## Tips for Perfect Ligurian Focaccia

Achieving that authentic Ligurian focaccia requires attention to a few key details:

*   **Quality Olive Oil is Non-Negotiable:** This cannot be stressed enough. A bland olive oil will result in bland focaccia. Use your best extra virgin olive oil.
*   **High Hydration:** Don't be scared of the sticky dough. A wet dough is crucial for the airy, open crumb. Resist adding extra flour during mixing.
*   **Gentle Handling:** Treat the dough gently, especially after the first rise. You want to preserve all those precious air bubbles.
*   **Ample Proofing:** Give the dough enough time to rise. This develops flavor and texture.
*   **Deep Dimples:** Really press your fingers into the dough to create those deep wells. They're essential for holding the olive oil and brine, which keeps the focaccia moist and flavorful.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt:** Use a good quality coarse sea salt for the topping. It provides a wonderful textural contrast and bursts of flavor.
*   **Don't Overbake:** Focaccia Genovese should be golden, not dark brown. Overbaking will dry it out.
*   **Pan Choice:** A dark metal baking pan often yields a better-crisped bottom crust.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

The beauty of Focaccia Genovese is its versatility. This authentic Focaccia Genovese Recipe is perfect for any time of day.

*   **Breakfast/Snack:** Enjoy a warm slice with your morning coffee, just like they do in Genoa.
*   **Appetizer:** Serve alongside a charcuterie board with cured meats, cheeses (like stracchino or fresh mozzarella), and olives.
*   **Dipping:** Cut into squares and serve with a small bowl of your finest extra virgin olive oil for dipping.
*   **Sandwiches:** Slice horizontally and fill with pesto, tomatoes, fresh basil, and mozzarella for a simple Caprese focaccia sandwich, or with prosciutto and arugula.
*   **Accompaniment:** It makes a wonderful side to soups, salads, or any Italian main course.
*   **Wine Pairing:** A crisp Ligurian white wine like Vermentino or Pigato complements the focaccia beautifully.

## Storage and Reheating Tips

Focaccia is always best enjoyed fresh, ideally within a few hours of baking when its crust is at its crispiest and its interior is at its most tender.

*   **Room Temperature:** If you have leftovers, store them in an airtight container or wrapped in foil at room temperature for up to 2 days. The crust will soften, but the flavor will remain.
*   **Freezing:** For longer storage, slice the cooled focaccia into individual portions and wrap each tightly in plastic wrap, then aluminum foil. Place in a freezer-safe bag for up to 1-2 months.
*   **Reheating:** To revive leftover focaccia, especially from the fridge or freezer, preheat your oven to 180°C (350°F). Place the focaccia directly on the oven rack or on a baking sheet and heat for 5-10 minutes (longer if frozen) until warmed through and the crust has crisped up again. A toaster oven also works well for smaller pieces.

## Variations on Focaccia Genovese

While the classic rosemary and olive oil Focaccia Genovese Recipe is perfect in its simplicity, there are delightful variations you can explore:

*   **Focaccia con le Cipolle (Onion Focaccia):** This is a very traditional Ligurian variation. Thinly slice white or red onions, sauté them gently until softened, and scatter them over the focaccia before baking.
*   **Cherry Tomato:** Halve small cherry tomatoes and press them into the dimples before baking for bursts of sweet acidity.
*   **Olives:** Pitted black or green olives, especially Taggiasca olives native to Liguria, can be nestled into the dough.
*   **Herbs:** Experiment with other fresh herbs like thyme or oregano, either alone or in combination with rosemary.
*   **Cheese:** A sprinkle of finely grated Parmesan cheese, or small dollops of creamy Stracchino cheese, can be added towards the end of baking.
*   **Garlic:** Thinly sliced garlic or garlic confit can be added with the rosemary for an extra aromatic kick.

No matter how you choose to enjoy it, the authentic Focaccia Genovese Recipe is a comforting and profoundly satisfying baking project that brings the authentic taste of Italy right into your home. Its irresistible aroma, incredible texture, and simple, pure flavors make it a true culinary delight.
